---
title: "Exercise: Hidden Markov Models by hand"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

After this exercise you will be able to:

- Identify the four ingredients of an HMM (states, transition matrix $A$,
  emission matrix $B$, initial distribution $\boldsymbol{\pi}$) in a biology
  problem.
- Tell the **generative story** of a 2-state HMM and **compute the probability
  of one specific hidden path together with its observations — by hand**.
- Compute a small **likelihood** by summing over a few paths.
- Reason about **decoding** (which of two paths is more likely).
- Check your by-hand numbers in R.

The by-hand path-probability calculation is the single most exam-relevant skill
of this block, so do the pen-and-paper parts *before* you touch R.

## Background

A **Hidden Markov Model** has a hidden Markov chain over states (the Day-7
chain) plus an **emission** at every step. We use:

- hidden states with **transition matrix** $A$, where
  $A_{ij}=P(z_{t+1}=j\mid z_t=i)$ and each **row sums to 1**;
- **emission matrix** $B$, where $B_{ik}=P(x_t=k\mid z_t=i)$, one row per
  state, one column per symbol, each **row sums to 1**;
- **initial distribution** $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ with $\pi_i=P(z_1=i)$.

The **joint probability** of a hidden path $\mathbf{z}=(z_1,\dots,z_T)$ together
with the observations $\mathbf{x}=(x_1,\dots,x_T)$ is the product along the
generative story:

$$
P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})
= \pi_{z_1}\, B_{z_1, x_1}
  \prod_{t=2}^{T} A_{z_{t-1}, z_t}\, B_{z_t, x_t}.
$$

In words: **start probability**, then alternate **emit** ($B$) and **move**
($A$), ending on an emission. The **likelihood** of the data alone sums this
over *every* possible path:
$P(\mathbf{x})=\sum_{\mathbf{z}}P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})$.

### The model for this exercise

States $\{H, L\}$: **H** = "GC-rich (CpG-island-like)", **L** = "normal
background". Symbols are the DNA bases $\{A, C, G, T\}$.

$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}=\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
A=\begin{array}{cc}
 & \begin{array}{cc} H & L \end{array}\\
\begin{array}{c} H\\ L \end{array} &
\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0.8 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{array}\right]
\end{array},
\qquad
B=
\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
0.1 & 0.4 & 0.4 & 0.1 \\
0.3 & 0.2 & 0.2 & 0.3
\end{array}\right]
\begin{array}{c} H\\ L \end{array}
$$

(The columns of $B$ are, in order, $A, C, G, T$.)

## By hand

Do these on paper. Show every factor in your products, not just the final
number.

### Problem 1 — read the model

(a) What does $B_{H,G}=0.4$ mean in words? What does $A_{L,L}=0.7$ mean?
(b) Verify that every row of $A$ and every row of $B$ sums to 1, and that
$\boldsymbol{\pi}$ sums to 1.
(c) Which state is more likely to emit a `C`? Which state tends to produce long
runs of the same state? (Look at the diagonal of $A$.)

### Problem 2 — probability of one path with its emissions

Observed sequence $\mathbf{x}=(G, C, A)$. Consider the hidden path
$\mathbf{z}=(H, H, L)$.

(a) Write out the product $P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})$ factor by factor, labelling
each factor as a start, an emission, or a transition.
(b) Evaluate it to a number.

### Problem 3 — compare two paths (decoding intuition)

Same observations $\mathbf{x}=(G, C, A)$. Now consider the all-high path
$\mathbf{z}'=(H, H, H)$.

(a) Compute $P(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}')$.
(b) Which of $\mathbf{z}=(H,H,L)$ and $\mathbf{z}'=(H,H,H)$ is the more likely
explanation of the data? This is the kind of comparison the **Viterbi**
algorithm makes (over all paths at once).

### Problem 4 — a small likelihood (summing over paths)

Take the **shorter** observation $\mathbf{x}=(G, C)$ (length 2).

(a) List **all** $2^2=4$ hidden paths.
(b) Compute $P(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$ for each path.
(c) Add them up to get the likelihood $P(\mathbf{x}=(G,C))$. (This is what the
**forward** algorithm computes, but for length 2 we can just enumerate.)

## In R

Use R only to **verify** the by-hand numbers. We are not using the `HMM`
package yet (that is Day 10) — here we just multiply the probabilities
ourselves so the arithmetic is transparent.

```r
# The model
pi  <- c(H = 0.5, L = 0.5)

A <- matrix(c(0.8, 0.2,
              0.3, 0.7), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
            dimnames = list(c("H", "L"), c("H", "L")))

# columns are A, C, G, T
B <- matrix(c(0.1, 0.4, 0.4, 0.1,
              0.3, 0.2, 0.2, 0.3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
            dimnames = list(c("H", "L"), c("A", "C", "G", "T")))

# Problem 2: path (H, H, L) with observations (G, C, A)
p_HHL <- pi["H"] * B["H", "G"] * A["H", "H"] * B["H", "C"] *
         A["H", "L"] * B["L", "A"]
p_HHL
# expected: 0.00384

# Problem 3: path (H, H, H) with the same observations
p_HHH <- pi["H"] * B["H", "G"] * A["H", "H"] * B["H", "C"] *
         A["H", "H"] * B["H", "A"]
p_HHH
# compare p_HHH with p_HHL: which path is more likely?
```

```r
# Problem 4: likelihood of x = (G, C) by enumerating all 4 paths
states <- c("H", "L")
obs    <- c("G", "C")

joint <- function(z1, z2) {
  pi[z1] * B[z1, obs[1]] * A[z1, z2] * B[z2, obs[2]]
}

paths <- expand.grid(z1 = states, z2 = states, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
paths$prob <- mapply(joint, paths$z1, paths$z2)
paths
sum(paths$prob)   # the likelihood P(x = (G, C))
```

The functions used: [`matrix`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matrix.html) (note
`byrow = TRUE` and `dimnames` so we can index by state/base names),
[`expand.grid`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/expand.grid.html) to list all paths, and
[`mapply`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/mapply.html) to apply the joint-probability
function to each path.

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — counting the factors"}
For a sequence of length $T$, the product $P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})$ has exactly
**one** $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ factor, **$T$** emission factors (one per position),
and **$T-1$** transition factors (one per gap between positions). For $T=3$
that is $1 + 3 + 2 = 6$ factors.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — the order of factors"}
Lay it out left to right as the generative story:
$$
\pi_{z_1}\;\to\; B_{z_1,x_1}\;\to\; A_{z_1,z_2}\;\to\; B_{z_2,x_2}
\;\to\; A_{z_2,z_3}\;\to\; B_{z_3,x_3}.
$$
Start, emit, move, emit, move, emit. Always finish on an emission.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — reading the matrices"}
$A_{ij}$ is the entry in **row** $i$ (current state), **column** $j$ (next
state). $B_{ik}$ is **row** $i$ (state), **column** $k$ (emitted symbol). For
$B$, the columns are ordered $A, C, G, T$, so e.g. $B_{H,G}$ is row $H$, third
column $=0.4$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — comparing two paths"}
For decoding you do **not** need the likelihood. Just compute the joint
probability of each candidate path and compare the numbers — the larger one is
the more likely explanation. (Viterbi does this comparison over *all* paths
simultaneously and efficiently.)
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 5 — likelihood vs path probability"}
A single path probability is $P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})$. The **likelihood**
$P(\mathbf{x})$ is the **sum** of those over every possible path. With 2 states
and length 2 there are $2^2=4$ paths: $(H,H), (H,L), (L,H), (L,L)$.
:::

## Solutions

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}

### Problem 1 — read the model

(a) $B_{H,G}=0.4$: *given* the hidden state is $H$ (GC-rich), the probability of
emitting a `G` is 0.4. $A_{L,L}=0.7$: given the chain is in state $L$, the
probability the next state is again $L$ is 0.7.

(b) Rows of $A$: $0.8+0.2=1$, $0.3+0.7=1$. Rows of $B$: $0.1+0.4+0.4+0.1=1$,
$0.3+0.2+0.2+0.3=1$. $\boldsymbol{\pi}$: $0.5+0.5=1$. All valid.

(c) `C` is more likely from $H$ ($B_{H,C}=0.4 > B_{L,C}=0.2$). State $H$ tends
to produce longer runs, since $A_{H,H}=0.8 > A_{L,L}=0.7$ (the larger diagonal
entry is "stickier").

### Problem 2 — one path with its emissions

Path $\mathbf{z}=(H,H,L)$, observations $\mathbf{x}=(G,C,A)$:

$$
P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})
= \underbrace{\pi_H}_{0.5}\;
  \underbrace{B_{H,G}}_{0.4}\;
  \underbrace{A_{H,H}}_{0.8}\;
  \underbrace{B_{H,C}}_{0.4}\;
  \underbrace{A_{H,L}}_{0.2}\;
  \underbrace{B_{L,A}}_{0.3}
$$
$$
= 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.8 \times 0.4 \times 0.2 \times 0.3 = \mathbf{0.003840}.
$$

### Problem 3 — compare two paths

Path $\mathbf{z}'=(H,H,H)$, same observations:
$$
P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z}')
= \pi_H\,B_{H,G}\,A_{H,H}\,B_{H,C}\,A_{H,H}\,B_{H,A}
= 0.5\times0.4\times0.8\times0.4\times0.8\times0.1 = \mathbf{0.005120}.
$$

Compare: $P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z}')=0.00512 > P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})=0.00384$.
So **the all-high path $(H,H,H)$ is the more likely explanation** of
$(G,C,A)$. Intuition: staying in $H$ is cheap (transition 0.8 vs 0.2), and even
though $H$ emits `A` with only 0.1, that loss is outweighed by the much higher
transition probability of staying put.

### Problem 4 — likelihood of $\mathbf{x}=(G,C)$

The four paths and their joint probabilities
$P=\pi_{z_1}\,B_{z_1,G}\,A_{z_1,z_2}\,B_{z_2,C}$:

| path | $\pi$ | emit $G$ | move | emit $C$ | product |
|--|--|--|--|--|--|
| $(H,H)$ | 0.5 | 0.4 | 0.8 | 0.4 | $0.0640$ |
| $(H,L)$ | 0.5 | 0.4 | 0.2 | 0.2 | $0.0080$ |
| $(L,H)$ | 0.5 | 0.2 | 0.3 | 0.4 | $0.0120$ |
| $(L,L)$ | 0.5 | 0.2 | 0.7 | 0.2 | $0.0140$ |

Likelihood:
$$
P(\mathbf{x}=(G,C)) = 0.0640 + 0.0080 + 0.0120 + 0.0140 = \mathbf{0.098}.
$$

### R verification

```r
p_HHL
# H
# 0.00384
p_HHH
# H
# 0.00512        # the all-H path is more likely, matching Problem 3

paths
#   z1 z2  prob
# 1  H  H 0.064
# 2  L  H 0.012
# 3  H  L 0.008
# 4  L  L 0.014
sum(paths$prob)
# [1] 0.098       # matches the by-hand likelihood
```

:::

## Where this leads

You computed path probabilities, a likelihood, and a path comparison **by
hand** for a tiny model. Real genomes have millions of positions and far too
many paths to enumerate. **Day 10** introduces the CRAN
[`HMM`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=HMM) package, which does all of this
efficiently: `forward` for the likelihood, `viterbi` to decode the most likely
island map, and `baumWelch` to *learn* $A$, $B$, $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ from
unlabelled sequence. After the linear-algebra block, these probabilistic-model
ideas return throughout the course — in the Bayesian-statistics block (latent
variables and inference), and again when we segment and annotate single-cell
data.
